text.title: Go handler struct pattern: DI, request DTOs, RFC 7807 error mapping
text.summary: Handlers as methods on a dependency-injecting struct, with request DTOs using binding/validate tags, typed response structs, and RFC 7807 error mapping instead of raw err.Error() in JSON.

rule.statement: Define handlers as methods on a struct that holds service dependencies (logger, services). Never use package-level globals (var DB *sql.DB) inside handlers. Inject dependencies at construction time.
rule.rationale: Package-level globals prevent parallel test execution (shared state between test cases) and make dependency substitution impossible without init() hacks.

rule.statement: Use c.ShouldBindJSON (not c.Bind) on Gin. ShouldBind returns an error without writing a response; c.Bind writes a 400 directly and the handler continues — producing a double-write bug.
rule.rationale: c.Bind's auto-response behavior is a Gin anti-pattern; handlers that check err after c.Bind may still execute, producing conflicting response writes.

rule.statement: Pass r.Context() (or c.Request.Context() in Gin) as the first argument into every service call. Never use context.Background() inside a handler.
rule.rationale: context.Background() ignores client disconnection. Long-running service calls (DB, HTTP) continue consuming resources after the client has gone away.
